svg.text: EGYPTIAN ARABIC
SCRIPT — TWO ALPHABETS, ONE LAND
EGYPTIAN ARABIC SCRIPT
Written in Arabic Perso-Arabic abjad; right-to-left; 28 letters
Same script as Modern Standard Arabic (MSA); dialect is spoken, not usually written
Formal writing: MSA • Social media, texts: Egyptian Colloquial Arabic
When written informally: same alphabet, dialectal spellings
EGYPTIAN ARABIC IN LATIN SCRIPT
Egyptian Arabic romanization: common online, no official standard
Numbers-in-text: internet convention for Semitic sounds lacking Latin letters
COPTIC SCRIPT: ANCESTOR IN EGYPT
Coptic: Greek alphabet + 6-7 Demotic Egyptian letters
Used to write Ancient Egyptian in Christian era (2nd-17th century CE)
Still used in Coptic Christian liturgy: ~10% of Egypt is Coptic Christian
SAMPLE EGYPTIAN ARABIC
Izzayak? "How are you?" (masc.) • Kwayyis, shukran "Fine, thanks"
Sabah el-kheir "Good morning" • Maassalama "Goodbye"
GREEK · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
GREEK ALPHABET
24 letters; left-to-right (opposite direction to Arabic)
Greek alphabet ~800 BCE: adapted from Phoenician, added vowel letters
First fully voweled alphabet in history (Phoenician was consonantal)
Greek letters became ancestor of Latin, Cyrillic, Coptic scripts
GREEK IN EGYPT: HISTORICAL ROLE
Alexander the Great conquered Egypt 332 BCE: Greek became official language
Ptolemaic dynasty (~305-30 BCE): pharaohs wrote in Greek
Rosetta Stone (196 BCE): same decree in hieroglyphs, Demotic, and Greek
SCRIPT COMPARISON
Arabic: right-to-left abjad (consonants only, vowels optional)
Greek: left-to-right full alphabet (all vowels written)
Coptic: bridge script (Greek alphabet + Egyptian sounds) used in Egypt
Egypt hosted all three script families: Semitic, Indo-European, hybrid Coptic
PLATE 60a ALT · EGYPTIAN ARABIC / GREEK · SCRIPT
